entry.title: Where ORD Is Used in SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud & SAP Databricks
entry.content:
SAP Open Resource Discovery (ORD) is becoming a foundational standard across the SAP ecosystem because it provides a consistent, self‑describing metadata layer for APIs, events, business objects, and data products. This metadata standardization is what enables smooth interoperability across SAP’s modern data and integration platforms.

Below are the key places where ORD is used today — and how it empowers seamless integration, especially with SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Databricks.

1. SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

SAP Business Accelerator Hub uses ORD as one of the primary mechanisms for publishing APIs and events. Any API showing an ORD ID on its overview page is officially ORD-enabled, meaning it exposes standardized metadata that can be consumed by SAP tools and data platforms.

This ensures:

  • Consistent API descriptions
  • Discoverability by SAP’s central aggregators
  • Compatibility with downstream systems like SAP Business Data Cloud
This is the first indicator that an API can participate in the broader data product ecosystem. 
 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

SAP uses ORD internally to improve SAP-to-SAP integration and provide a unified metadata layer that development tools (like SAP Business Application Studio) can consume for enhanced API discovery and automation.

Each Product Owner from SAP Products LoB will be develop, design ORD to make it available in SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) and Data Products Marketplace for you..

3. SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)

SAP Business Data Cloud actively uses ORD to describe Data Products and make them discoverable to consumers across the SAP ecosystem, including SAP Datasphere. ORD helps BDC deliver a unified catalog with:

  • Descriptions of datasets
  • Relationships
  • Capabilities
  • Higher-level metadata structures like data products and business entities
In addition, third‑party systems such as SAP Databricks are required to describe data products using ORD before publishing them into SAP Business Data Cloud.
 
This ensures smooth catalog integration and consistent metadata across SAP and non-SAP data sources.
 

4. SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere consumes data products published to SAP Business Data Cloud — and because BDC requires ORD‑based metadata, Datasphere gains:

  • Automatically discoverable data products
  • Standardized metadata for modeling
  • Compatibility with external data such as Databricks Delta Lake

Datasphere uses ORD indirectly because it relies on BDC’s ORD-driven catalog for understanding and federating data assets.

Additionally, SAP’s hybrid integration patterns show that Datasphere can integrate with Databricks using JDBC or federated models — and ORD ensures data products between these systems are consistently described.

5. SAP Databricks (Enterprise Databricks Integration)

SAP Databricks integrates deeply with SAP Business Data Cloud using BDC Connect, enabling bi-directional sharing of data products. To publish a data product from SAP Databricks to SAP Business Data Cloud:

  • The metadata must be described using ORD and CSN (Core Schema Notation)
  • The data product becomes discoverable to SAP systems (e.g., Datasphere) via BDC

Furthermore, Databricks–SAP integration uses Delta Share, JDBC, and federated models, but ORD plays the critical role of standardizing data product metadata.

This creates a smooth, unified integration path:

SAP applications → ORD-based metadata → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere & Databricks

SAP Architecture Center also confirms this hybrid pattern where business data from SAP and non‑SAP (Databricks) is harmonized through Business Data Cloud and Datasphere, enabling interoperable AI/ML and analytics scenarios.

 Refer my SAP blog

6. Data Products Across the SAP Ecosystem

ORD also provides standardized metadata for:

  • Entity Types / Business Objects
  • APIs and Events
  • Data Products 
This consistency is the key enabler for cross‑platform interoperability — especially as Data Products become central to the SAP ecosystem via Business Data Cloud and Datasphere.

Summary: How ORD Enables Smooth Integration Across SAP & Databricks

With ORD in place:

  • APIs with an ORD ID on api.sap.com can be safely consumed to build data products in SAP Business Data Cloud.
  • Business Data Cloud uses ORD metadata to catalog and serve data products to SAP Datasphere and SAP Databricks.
  • SAP Databricks uses ORD when publishing its own data products back into Business Data Cloud.
  • SAP Datasphere leverages ORD-driven metadata to discover, model, and federate data products seamlessly.

ORD is the metadata “glue” that ensures smooth integration across:

SAP BTP → Business Accelerator Hub → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere → Databricks

And the more SAP and partner solutions adopt ORD, the smoother, more automated, and more scalable integrations become across the SAP landscape.

entry.title: Blog Series: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting
entry.content:
Most enterprises still operate in a world of dashboards that simply report what happened. KPIs move, charts update, and alerts fire when thresholds are crossed — yet none of this tells us whether the deviation is expected, normal for this period, or a real operational risk.

With AI, reporting can evolve from passive visualization to active, context‑aware decisioning.
Instead of waiting for users to interpret numbers, the system itself can observe, understand context, reason over patterns, decide significance, and take action.

This is Agentic Reporting — where insights behave like a junior analyst embedded inside your SAP landscape (S/4HANA, SAC, Datasphere, BTP), continuously interpreting changes and surfacing only what truly needs attention.

What Makes Reporting Agentic? 

Traditional reporting is static: 

  • Predefined KPIs 
  • Rigid thresholds 
  • IF → THEN rules 
  • No learning  

Agentic reporting is dynamic: 

  •  Observes data continuously 
  • Considers context — seasonality, patterns, user role, business calendar 
  •  Determines if a deviation is meaningful 
  • Recommends or triggers action 
  • Learns from outcomes  

Why Agents > Classic Alerts/Rigid Triggers 

Reporting Triggers: 

  • Static 
  • One KPI - One Action
  • Same action every time 
  • Push Only
  • No Context, No learning  

AI Agents: 

  • Adaptive, context-aware thresholds 
  • Understands correlations across KPIs 
  • Chooses the optimal action 
  • Learns over time 
  • Proactive + conversational 

 

Agentic Reporting Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring

Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring Tool

Traditional Approach: 

  • “If DSO > 60 days → Send email.” 
  • No context → many false alarms. 

Agentic Approach: 

  • “Is this deviation abnormal given today’s reality?” 

 Scenario A – 8% DSO Increase (No Alert) 

Agent checks context

  • It’s the end of quarter (seasonal spike expected)
  • A major customer’s payment cycle always delays during this period
  • The same pattern occurred in the last 3 quarters

Conclusion: The deviation is expected and harmless → No alert raised.

 Scenario B – Same 8% Increase Next Month (Alert!) 

Agent checks context

  • No end of quarter ❌
  • No seasonal explanation ❌
  • pattern does not match past 3 quarters ❌
  • Impacts cashflow ❌
  • Unusual vs history ❌

Conclusion: The deviation is unexpected and risky → Escalate with analysis

 Agent provides: 

  • Root-cause patterns ✔️
  • Customer contribution ✔️
  • Impact on forecast ✔️
  • Recommended actions ✔️

 The Bottom Line 

Agentic Reporting takes organizations from: 

Dashboards you look at → Dashboards that look out for you. 

 As SAP deepens Joule, AI, and agentic capabilities, this shift becomes not only possible — but necessary.

Next in the series:

  • Blog #2: Two Ways to Build Agentic Reporting in SAP: Python Agent on SAP BTP vs. Joule Studio Agent
  • Blog #3: How to Build a Python Agent for Agentic Reporting (Datasphere + BTP)
  • Blog #4: How to Build an SAP-native Joule Agent for Agentic Reporting

entry.title: SAML Based Single Sign-On Setup with SAP Identity Authentication Service
entry.content:
Introduction

About SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere is SAP’s unified data service for integrating, modeling and governing data across hybrid and cloud environments. As part of SAP’s Business Technology Platform (BTP), it supports enterprise-scale analytics, governance and collaboration.

 

Need for SSO

Traditional password-based authentication poses risks such as credential sprawl and inconsistent access control. Implementing SSO in Datasphere enhances:

  •              Security: Centralized identity and access management.
  •              User Experience: Seamless access without repetitive logins.
  •              Compliance: Alignment with enterprise security and governance standards.
  •              Efficiency: Reduced administrative overhead for user provisioning and password resets.

 

Architecture Overview

SSO in SAP Datasphere is typically implemented using SAML 2.0 (Security Assertion Markup Language) through SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS).

Components Involved

  • SAP Datasphere Tenant: The target system for SSO-enabled access.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services: Identity Authentication (IAS): Acts as the trusted authentication broker.
  • Corporate Identity Provider (IdP): Provides user authentication.
  • SAP BTP Subaccount: Links Datasphere to IAS through service bindings.

 

Authentication Flow

 

Prerequisites

Before enabling SSO, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. SAP Datasphere Tenant - Provisioned and accessible via SAP BTP
  2. SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) - Tenant available and linked to BTP subaccount
  3. Corporate Identity Provider - Configured to support SAML 2.0
  4. Admin Access - Administrator roles in Datasphere, IAS and IdP
  5. Email Domain - Verified in IAS for trusted domain mapping

You must have the system owner role for your SAP Datasphere tenant to perform this configuration.

 

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Access IAS Tenant

  • Log in to your SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication admin console.
  • Under Applications & Resources → Applications, create a new application named “SAP Datasphere Test”.

Step 2: Download Metadata

  • Login to Datasphere tenant: System -> Administration -> Security
  • Authentication Method: SAML Single Sign On (SSO)
  • SAML Single Sign On (SSO) Configuration: Step1 - Download Service Provider Metadata

Step 3: Configure SAML Settings in IAS

  • SAML 2.0 Configuration: Define from Metadata -> Upload the metadata downloaded from Datasphere tenant

  • Subject Name Identifier

Source - Identity Directory and Value - Email

  • Attributes:

Maintain below values in Self-defined attributes.

For SAML attributes to be recognized it is also important that the “Groups” attribute contains the value of “sac”.

  • Conditional Authentication

Select “Default Identity Provider” - > Your IDP

“Check” the checkbox – Allow users stored in Identity Authentication service to login.

  • Download Metadata file from SAML 2.0 Configuration:

Step 4: Configure Trust in Datasphere

  1. In SAP Datasphere → System → Administration → Security Settings → Identity Provider Configuration.
  2. Upload Identity Provider Metadata -> Upload.

     3. Choose a user Attribute to map to your identity provider -> Email. It should map to User ID, Email or a custom attribute. The attribute will be used to map users from your existing SAML user list to SAP Datasphere. The selected user attribute must match the NameID (Subject Name Identifier) used in your custom SAML assertion.

4. Confirm that the mapping is working -> Verify Account

Copy the URL and open it in a private/incognito browser window to avoid reusing an existing session and ensure you are prompted for a fresh login.

Upon successful verification, the Login Credential field will be highlighted with a green border. After confirming this, select “Convert” to complete the SAML SSO configuration.

 

Step 5: Test Authentication

  1. Access SAP Datasphere via SSO-enabled URL.
  2. Verify that users are redirected to IAS and authenticated by the corporate IdP. 
  3. Ensure user attributes and roles are correctly mapped in Datasphere.

Best Practices

  • Use HTTPS for all communications between systems.
  • Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the corporate IdP level.
  • Periodically rotate SAML certificates. 
  • Maintain audit logs in IAS for compliance.

Conclusion

Enabling SSO in SAP Datasphere strengthens enterprise security while enhancing usability and operational efficiency. Centralizing authentication through IAS and your corporate identity provider reduces the risk of credential misuse, simplifies onboarding, and improves user productivity. As organizations continue to adopt cloud and hybrid data platforms, a well-governed identity strategy becomes essential. This SSO implementation helps establish that foundation and supports future growth and integration initiatives.

SAP Sources: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/9f804b8efa8043539289f42f372c4862/9b26536159354aea9024a99cbbe60b4e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4477056515.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: SAP DSP – Automated Test Tool
entry.content:
Minor changes in sub-views can have huge impact on dependent Views (Model-Views/FACT-Level), Models, SAC Stories. Nothing new, but “Lernt Durch Schmerz” I found that even the approach: Test-Fail-Build-Test will not help. Full regression testing is required as unexpected dependency can cause huge data issues. As with all projects, time pressure may not allow full regression testing and a single developer, nor the Product Owner, will know the entire system / impact of the requested changes.

Testing by the end-user should be the last Option.

Development in SAC is more application oriented and as of now I am not aware of a way to fully test SAC Stories from functional and data side. Alternative would be to use SAC Excel-Addin to download snapshots of AMs and compare before and after the changes were made. For this alternative VBA scripting would be required and I am not sure if fully automated Excel / Workbook based Test can be done easily. Therefore, here is a proposal for DSP based Automated Test Tool supporting CD/CI approach.

Objects Used:

Type

Usage

Table

Snapshot table

Table View (private-like/base)

Dimension view based on the Snapshot table

Model-View (query-like/consumption)

FACT-Level View consuming the Table view

Model

Model used in the Story

FACT-Level View (query-like/consumption)

Object to be regression tested

KPI Generator View

Snapshot generator for each Object to be regression tested

Data Flow

Pushing Snapshots to Snapshot table

Task Chain

Executing the Data Flow/s

Story

Evaluating the data

 

How-To:

We have a limitation in DSP, that only Views can be used in Data Flows. Therefore only base-line Measures can be used as of now. Base-line Measures test will catch 80-90% of issues, however if this limitation is a problem, an alternative would be to use OData on Models and use Azure f.E. as base for the tool.

1. Create the Snapshot table: I used Provider (I am from BW world), Date, Time, Version, KPI and Value. I omitted Unit as I select the Unit in the KPI Generator View.

2. Define the KPIs to be regression tested: selection on these KPIs should be in the past (last year f.e.) ensuring stable data. Most critical / most used base-measures should be selected as KPIs. Even Row-Count can be considered in case master data is changed (f.e. time-dependency is introduced).

3. Create the KPI Generator View: based on the chosen KPIs simply aggregate the results with dedicated KPI naming (f.E. NetRevenue, ...).

4. Create Data Flow: which will just push the Snapshots into the Snapshots table.

5. Create Taks Chain, Model, Story: for easier consumption / evaluation of the snapshots. 

Approach on DEV System (optional):  

  1. Create Snapshots via Task Chain scheduled every morning / evening
  2. Have the developers check on their own

Approach on TST System:

  1. Create Snapshots via Task Chain run before the transport
  2. Import all objects
  3. Create Snapshots via Task Chain run after the transport
  4. Compare before and after transport Snapshots
  5. All differences must be explainable

 

Snapshot table:

Field

Usage

PROVIDER

FACT-Level View / Tested Object unique ID

DATE

Snapshot date

TIME

Snapshot time

VERSION

Usage (optional)

KPI

The KPI to be tested

Value

Value

 

Dimension view based on the Snapshot table:

SELECT * FROM [SNAPSHOT TABLE]

Optional. I never directly use tables. Learned that from my BW experience developing Extractors on the ECC side.

 

FACT-Level View consuming the Table view:

In our case SELECT * FROM [SNAPSHOT VIEW], but FACT-Level to be used as Model-View. Can be enhanced with Associations f.E.

 

Model used in the Story:

To be used in the Story for easier evaluation.

 

Snapshot generator for each Object to be regression tested:

In this view, the KPIs and Provider are defined. Basically the most-used / most-critical base-measures. For each object tested, one Snapshot generator view should be created. Alternatively one view could be created, but having hundreds of SQL lines is not the best approach.

This is just an example view:

 

Data Flow Pushing Snapshots to Snapshot table:

 

Task Chain:

Just executing the Data Flow/s

 

Story:

 

Basically comparing Snapshots before and after. Basic testing approach, just allowing to be automated and covering all critical Models.

Main blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/bw-vs-datasphere-dsp-amp-sac/ba-p/1428...

entry.title: Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
entry.content:
As your organisation prepares to adopt SAP Datasphere, the comprehensive data fabric solution as part of SAP Business Data Cloud, it is always recommended to carefully plan configuration and optimization to deliver its full potential. Often the complexity of initial deployment can prove challenging, even for experienced IT teams. This is where SAP's Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services become a strategic asset.

The SAP Foundational Success Plan, as part of SAP's Services and Support portfolio, provides essential onboarding, technical cloud operations and preventive support included with every cloud solution from SAP. Our SAP Enterprise Support service and support offerings are built into the solution subscription at no additional cost. 
Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services are a key part of this offering.

Proactive Risk Mitigation

The CQC Deployment Readiness for SAP Datasphere is designed to identify potential issues before they impact your production environment. By conducting a thorough review of your tenant configuration, SAP experts can detect risks early and address them before go-live, when remediation would otherwise be significantly more costly and disruptive.
To support customers beyond the initial deployment, we also offer the CQC Solution Optimization for SAP Datasphere.
While Deployment Readiness focuses on preparing your SAP Datasphere environment for a successful go-live, the Solution Optimization service supports the run phase by helping ensure ongoing system health, optimize system operations, and provide clear mitigation guidance. Together, these complementary services provide proactive support both before and after go-live, enabling customers to maintain a stable, optimized environment and build a strong foundation for continuous innovation within SAP Business Data Cloud.
These checks include:

  • Data Provisioning Agent setup,
  • Space management,
  • Data replication processes
  • Details of underlying SAP HANA Cloud database metrics
  • and more...


Alignment with Best Practices

One of the core strengths of these Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services is their foundation in SAP best practices. The predefined scope and checks provide a consistent and comprehensive assessment of critical areas that directly impact system adoption and reliability, allowing customers to benefit from SAP’s extensive experience across many implementations rather than relying on trial and error.
The CQC for Deployment Readiness is most valuable before business go-live, offering a holistic baseline assessment to validate system readiness and identify potential risks early. Complementing this, the CQC for Solution Optimization supports customers after go-live by helping maintain system health, identify improvement opportunities, and ensure the environment remains optimized during the run phase. Together, these services provide proactive guidance throughout key stages of the solution lifecycle.


Timing and Preparation

To maximize the value of these services and allow sufficient time to act on recommendations, SAP recommends the following timelines when requesting them:

  • Deployment Readiness – Request this service approximately six weeks before your planned go-live date. This provides adequate time to review the findings, implement recommended improvements, and validate the changes before entering production.
  • Solution Optimization – Request this service approximately four weeks prior to your desired delivery date and ensure it is scheduled for the productive tenant. This allows SAP experts to assess the live environment and provide targeted recommendations to maintain system health and optimize your system operations.

 

➡️ These services can be requested through the Get Support application in SAP for Me.
➡️ Full details of the Deployment Readiness service can be found in this infosheet.
➡️ Full details of the Solution Optimization service can be found in this infosheet.
➡️ For more information on Continuous Quality Checks please visit Continuous Quality Check Overview

For organizations deploying SAP Datasphere as part of SAP Business Data Cloud, these CQC services provide proactive guidance along your solution lifecycle (both before and after go-live), ensuring alignment to SAP best practices and accelerating time-to-value for your data management initiatives, deployment success, and continuous innovation.

 


GET MORE CONTENT LIKE THIS!

SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for foundational support and empowerment — have officially moved to a dedicated SAP Community Group.

As part of your SAP cloud subscription, you get access to:

  • Expert-led resources
  • Tailored recommendations
  • Advisory services
  • …all designed to help you accelerate innovation and success.

👉 Visit our Community Group
Sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID, then hit “Subscribe” to stay updated.

Looking to dive deeper into data-driven innovation?
Within the group, explore the Data & Analytics Value Map to connect with your Foundational Support team. Simply click “Join Group”, and once inside, you can even request a call with a topic expert to get personalized guidance and enablement support.

entry.title: Two Ways to Build Agentic Reporting in SAP: Python Agent on SAP BTP vs. Joule Studio Agent
entry.content:
1. Introduction

In my previous blog post on Agentic Reporting, I talked about how modern reporting goes beyond dashboards — it requires systems that reason, explain, and act.

In this follow‑up, instead of going straight into a specific implementation, I want to answer a bigger question:

What are the different ways to build an AI Agent in the SAP ecosystem?

With SAP’s evolving AI landscape, you now have two powerful paths to build enterprise‑grade agents:

Option A — Build a Python Agent on SAP BTP

Custom, full‑code, flexible Agent service that integrates with SAP Datasphere via SQL or OData.

Option B — Build an Agent directly in SAP Joule Studio

A no‑code/low‑code agent builder inside SAP Build for reasoning, orchestration, and multi‑system actions.

Both options are valid. Both can support Agentic Reporting. But they differ significantly in architecture, flexibility, governance, and effort.

This post compares both, so you can choose the best path for your use case.

2. The Two Agent‑Building Paths Explained

Option A — Python Agent on SAP BTP (Full‑Code + Maximum Flexibility)

This is the classic approach where you build an application in Python, deploy it on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma), and connect it to SAP Datasphere.

When is this ideal?

  • Heavy data processing
  • Custom ML/AI logic
  • Integration with complex non-SAP systems
  • You want maximum control over logic, dependencies, libraries
  • Advanced reasoning models + custom frameworks
  • You want to host an API that other tools can call (including Joule)

What your Python Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP Datasphere using:
    • SQL (hdbcli)
    • OData Analytical or Relational APIs
  • Execute complex logic (anomaly detection, simulations, scoring)
  • Write data back to Open SQL schema
  • Expose REST endpoints for other SAP systems and Joule agents to consume

Architecture Summary

  • Python app on BTP
  • Service bindings → HANA Cloud credentials via VCAP_SERVICES
  • OAuth → Datasphere OData access
  • HANA Cloud SQL → read/write via Open SQL schema
  • Python performs reasoning, actions, decisions

Option B — Joule Studio Agent (SAP-native no‑code Agent Builder)

SAP Joule Studio allows business teams and architects to build agents without needing to write any code.

These agents can:

  • Plan
  • Reason
  • Execute workflows
  • Call APIs (via Joule Skills)
  • Use SAP Knowledge Graph
  • Use Document Grounding
  • Orchestrate SAP and non‑SAP tools

When is this ideal?

  • You want an SAP-native, governed agent
  • You want quick setup (no DevOps, no runtime)
  • You want conversational + actionable agents
  • You want orchestration across SAP applications
  • You want to avoid managing Python runtimes
  • You want automatic reasoning and traceability

What your Joule Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP systems via destinations
  • Invoke Joule Skills (API calls, BTP actions)
  • Consume Datasphere data via OData APIs
  • Perform reasoning using SAP’s LLMs
  • Automate workflows in SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, etc.

Architecture Summary

  • Joule Studio = Agent Builder
  • Uses Destinations for integration
  • Uses Skills & Actions for API calls
  • LLM-powered agentic reasoning

No deploy, no runtime maintenance

3. Comparing the Two Approaches

4. Which Option Should You Use?

When to Choose Python Agent on BTP

Technical Complexity Requirements:

  • Advanced algorithmic logic - When you need custom algorithms beyond standard business logic
  • Heavy transformations/ETL - For complex data processing workflows requiring full programmatic control 
  • External ML libraries - Integration with sklearn, PyTorch, transformers, or other specialized libraries 

Integration Scenarios:

  • API endpoint creation - When other systems need to call your agent as a service
  • Any system connectivity - Leveraging the "any API, any system" capability for non-SAP integrations 

When to Choose Joule Studio Agent

Rapid Development Needs:

  • Fast deployment with the "lower effort, no-code" approach 
  • Built-in agentic reasoning eliminating custom development overhead 

Enterprise Governance:

  • SAP-native governance & logging for compliance and auditability 
  • Built-in trace, executions, reasoning view for operational transparency 
  • Skill marketplace access for pre-built capabilities 

SAP Ecosystem Integration:

  • SAP-first integrations with S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, and Ariba 
  • SAP Knowledge Graph + Documents for automatic grounding 
  • Workflow automation & orchestration as the primary use case

5. Conclusion:

SAP’s Agentic AI is evolving rapidly, and you now have two mature ways of building enterprise‑grade agents:

1. Python Agent on SAP BTP

Flexible, powerful, code-based, perfect for analytics and complex logic.

2️. Joule Studio Agent

SAP-native, governed, no-code, perfect for orchestration, reasoning, and functional automation.

Both are valid choices and can even work together.

 

Next in the series:

  • Blog #3: How to Build a Python Agent for Agentic Reporting (Datasphere + BTP)
  • Blog #4: How to Build an SAP-native Joule Agent for Agentic Reporting

Previous Blog in the series:

Blog #1: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting

entry.title: Integrating SAP Databricks with SAP CPQ and SAP Datasphere for Analytics & Reporting
entry.content:
In today’s data-driven enterprises, sales teams rely on real-time insights to make faster and smarter decisions. Yet, many organizations still struggle with fragmented data landscapes—especially when dealing with complex systems like SAP CPQ (Configure Price Quote), SAP Datasphere, and advanced analytics platforms.

To solve this challenge, we designed an end‑to‑end architecture that seamlessly connects SAP CPQ, Databricks, and SAP Datasphere. The result? A unified, governed, and scalable analytics ecosystem that unlocks deeper sales insights.

This blog breaks down the business goals, architecture, and implementation blueprint behind the integration.

Birds High View. (Process View)

Why This Integration Matters

The core objective is simple but powerful:
Bring SAP CPQ quotation data into SAP Datasphere—through Databricks—to enable centralized reporting, analytics, and visualization.

This pipeline creates:

  • A single source of truth for CPQ data
  • Real‑time or near‑real‑time insights
  • A governed, secure, and scalable data platform
  • Smooth interoperability across SAP and non‑SAP systems

Business Requirements at a Glance

Functional Requirements

To support modern analytics use cases, the solution must:

  • Extract all sales quotation records from SAP CPQ via APIs
  • Store data in a governed, versioned Delta Lake format
  • Share data securely with SAP Datasphere
  • Enable full data lineage and governance
  • Support real-time or near-real-time use cases

Non‑Functional Requirements

Behind the scenes, the platform must also ensure:

  • Performance: Process incremental CPQ changes with <5‑minute latency
  • Security: Encryption and role‑based access control throughout
  • Scalability: Handle 100,000+ quotations per day
  • Compliance: GDPR and SOX adherence for sensitive financial data

Solution Architecture Overview

The integration spans four major layers: data sources, data platform, data sharing, and data consumption.

1. Data Sources

The journey begins with SAP CPQ, which exposes:

  • REST APIs for extracting quotation data
  • OAuth 2.0 authentication using client credentials

These APIs provide structured access to all quotation objects and line items.

2. Databricks – The Data Transformation Engine

Databricks acts as the data engineering and governance hub.

  • PySpark ETL notebooks orchestrate ingestion and transformation
  • Unity Catalog oversees centralized governance
  • Delta Lake ensures ACID transactions and version-controlled storage

This allows teams to build clean, enriched, analytics-ready datasets.

3. Data Sharing: Delta Sharing + SAP BDC

For secure, open data sharing, the architecture leverages:

  • Delta Sharing Protocol for exchanging datasets using industry standards
  • SAP BDC (Business Data Connectivity) for ORD-based integration into Datasphere

The combination ensures that SAP Datasphere receives fresh, trusted data with minimal friction.

4. Consumption Layer

Finally, business users analyze the curated CPQ data through:

  • SAP Datasphere for modeling and semantic layering
  • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for dashboards and reports

The result is a seamless analytics experience with governed enterprise data.

The Data Model: What’s Being Shared?

To enable deep sales analytics, the shared data model includes:

Key Entities

  • Quotations: Quote ID, Quote Status, Opportunity ID, Customer ID, Amount, Status, Quote Created Date
  • Line Items: Product ID, Quantity, Unit Price, Net Price, Target Price, Margins
  • Customers: Business Partners,  Region, Segment
  • Products: Product Name, Category, SKU

Relationships

  • Quotation → Line Items: One-to-many
  • Quotation → Customer: Many-to-one

This structure supports reporting such as win rate trends, regional sales patterns, product profitability, and more.

Integration Flows

1. Data Ingestion 

  • Frequency: Hourly incremental loads
  • One Example API Endpoint: /api/v1/quotes
  • Resilience: Retry logic with exponential backoff

2. Data Transformation in Databricks

Transformations include:

  • Cleansing and deduplication
  • Enriching quotations with master data
  • Creating derived metrics like:
    • Win Rate
    • Average Deal Size
    • Quote-to-close duration

3. Data Governance

To meet compliance and governance needs:

  • PII fields are tagged and masked
  • Row-level security applies by sales region
  • All schema changes are version controlled through Delta Lake

Security & Compliance Framework

The pipeline is designed with enterprise-grade security:

  • Authentication: Service principals for API access
  • Authorization: Fine-grained ACLs via Unity Catalog
  • Encryption: TLS for in-transit, AES‑256 for at-rest data
  • Auditability: Complete logging of data access and transformations

This ensures audit readiness for GDPR, SOX, and internal IT security checks.

How We Measure Success

To validate business adoption and technical reliability, we track:

Data Quality

99% accuracy of quotation data

Platform Availability

  • 99.9% uptime SLA for the pipeline

User Adoption

  • 50+ monthly active business users in SAP Analytics Cloud

entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extractors
entry.content:
This is part 3 of our blog series investigating how to reuse existing BW artefacts in BDC such as extractors and custom logic.

As organizations embrace cloud-native data architectures, the journey from SAP BW on HANA 7.5 to SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) represents a critical modernization path. Our team recently completed a pilot implementation focusing on first, lifting BW into SAP BDC and modernizing it using SAP Datasphere as the primary target architecture.

This blog post shares practical insights and key learnings from this experience, particularly around handling standard extractors and preserving custom extractors business logic in SAP BDC.

I. The Challenge: Standard Extractors in a Modern Architecture

Think of standard SAP extractors as old filing systems that worked perfectly in traditional warehouses but struggle in modern cloud environments. Standard SAP extractors (2LIS_02_ITM, 0CO_OM_CCA_9, 2LIS_03_BF) present significant migration challenges when moving from traditional BW environments to SAP Business Data Cloud. This was primarily due to missing primary key definitions. SAP has modernized these extractors through SAP Datasphere, which acts as an intelligent intermediary that handles the complex transformation from legacy extraction patterns to cloud-native data products, enabling faster, more reliable data flows without requiring extensive custom coding.

Implementation Reality

With delta processing already active and productive in your Datasphere environment, the three critical extractors now operate seamlessly: 2LIS_02_ITM delivers purchase order changes, 0CO_OM_CCA_9 streams cost center postings, and 2LIS_03_BF provides material movements for non-cumulative inventory analysis. Datasphere eliminates traditional complexity by managing initialization, delta processing, and data semantics automatically, transforming raw SAP tables into business-ready entities (Purchase Orders, Cost Centers, Stock) that SAP Analytics Cloud can consume directly for analytics and planning scenarios. The same entities can also by consumed directly by SAP HANA Cloud for custom AI / ML scenarios or by end-to-end Intelligent Applications.

Design implications now that delta handling works

With delta mode active in Datasphere, your design simplifies and becomes more “cloud-native”:

  1. Each extractor follows a clear pattern:
  • Run initialization and delta in Replication Flow in Datasphere

 

  • Configure the extractor “primary key” in the Configure schema

 

  • Enable scheduled delta loads (e.g., hourly, daily, near real time).

 

  • New data is created in your source system (ECC or S4)

 

  • Deltas are replicated in Datasphere (table is filtered for the specific PO that was newly created)
  1. Operational benefits:
  • No more large daily full loads.
  • Network and storage usage is reduced because only changes move.
  • Latency improves: BDC can be updated much more frequently.
  • Monitoring focuses on delta queues and extractor status in Datasphere.

Practical checklist for your migration

When running this in a real project with delta fully available:

  • Per extractor:
    • Confirm that initialization has been done successfully in Datasphere.
    • Ensure delta jobs are configured, scheduled, and monitored.
    • Align delta frequency with business needs (close to real time vs batch).
  • In Datasphere:
    • Model business keys clearly (e.g., PO number, item, company code; Controlling area, cost center, period).
    • Build semantic models (views, business entities) that hide technical complexity from BDC consumers.Stay tuned for more details on this topic in the next blog post.
    • Use lineage so others know which extractors feed which BDC data products.
  • In BDC:
    • Point your analytical models and stories to the Datasphere-based artifacts instead of direct raw tables.
    • Design calculations assuming data is continuously refreshed via delta.

Beyond standard extractors, the real differentiation in a BDC migration comes from how you bring your custom fields and business logic in extractor enhancements into the new landscape.

 

II. How to Handle Custom Fields and Extractor Extensions in SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere

Reusing a customer’s existing custom logic is essential in SAP because it preserves years of in-house business knowledge, avoids costly redevelopment, and accelerates modernization while keeping results from business outcomes without business disruption.

In our pilot, we showed how SAP Datasphere within SAP Business Data Cloud can consume SAP standard extractors enhanced by custom fields without redesigning the entire data flow from scratch. Using the 0FI_AP_4 extractor (which supports both full and delta loads) as an example, the steps below illustrate how to expose customized business rules in financial data end‑to‑end to BDC.

These steps are not meant to be a full tutorial on how to enhance a standard SAP extractor – that pattern has been around for decades – but we believe it is still useful to summarize the key activities here. We will also show how these enhanced extractors are replicated (initial load and delta) into SAP Datasphere, in SAP Business Data Cloud.

  1. Check Existing Fields and Enhancement Options
    1. In the source system, use RSA2 (or R0S2/RSA6 depending on release) to display DataSource 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Verify whether the fields you need are already present but hidden (this is common in standard extractors).
    3. If they are not available, plan to add them via an append structure and fill them using RSAP0001 or a BAdI implementation.
  2. Enhance the Extract Structure (Append Structure)
    1. Still in RSA2, navigate to the extract structure of 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Create an append structure and add your custom fields (usually prefixed with ZZ or YY).
    3. Assign correct data elements (taken from SE11 table definitions) and activate the append structure.

After activation, the new fields are physically part of the extract structure but not yet filled with data.

  1. Implement the Extractor Logic (User Exit / BAdI)
    1. Create or reuse an enhancement project in CMOD and assign enhancement RSAP0001.
    2. For transactional data, implement component EXIT_SAPLRSAP_001 (include ZXRSAU01) and write the ABAP logic that populates the new fields for DataSource 0FI_AP_4.
      • Use I_DATASOURCE in the exit to branch specifically on 0FI_AP_4.
      • Read the necessary tables (e.g. BSEG, BKPF or customer tables) with the SELECT statement and loop over the extraction internal table to fill the custom fields using field symbols.
    3. Activate the enhancement project and the include program.

(Alternative: in newer systems you can use the dedicated BAdI for DataSource enhancement instead of RSAP0001, the principle for the logic remains the same.)

  1. Unhide and Configure the Fields in RSA6
    1. Open RSA6 and choose the source system.
    2. Select DataSource 0FI_AP_4 in change mode.
    3. Locate the new fields and remove the Hide flag; if needed, adjust properties such as “field only known in exit”.
    4. Save and activate the DataSource so that the new fields become visible for extraction (RSA3) and for replication to BW/Datasphere.

If you extended an LO Cockpit DataSource, you might also need to check the extract structure and update/activate it via LBWE so that queue filling works correctly.

In our pilot, we have created a simple logic by assigning a single value (1000) to the controlling area.

  1. Replicate and Consume in SAP Datasphere within SAP BDC
    1. In SAP Datasphere, connect to the source system and activate the enhanced extractor 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Create a replication flow to run an initial full load and delta loads, now including the custom fields.
    3. Once the replication flow runs and creates the local table in Datasphere, the enhanced field has been replicated with its data automaticaly replicated.

This completes the chain from technical enhancement in S/4HANA (or ECC) to business‑ready data, analytics and AI in BDC, while following the standard BW enhancement process.

III. Next Steps: From data replication to answering business questions

Once extractors (standard and extended) are replicated into SAP Datasphere and exposed to BDC, the next step is to turn raw data into multi-dimensional models to provide data for analytical purposes to answer different business questions.

  1. In SAP Datasphere, build Analytic Models on top of the replicated tables and datasources, defining measures, dimensions and hierarchies that fulfill business requirements. The latest update on the data product generator allows to semantically import BW 7.5 PCE InfoProvider data (works like semantic onboarding but is triggered from BW side and pushed into the object store of SAP Datasphere).
  2. Publish these models as consumption artifacts (expose analytic models/views, create and share data products) that can be consumed by SAP Analytics Cloud , SAP HANA Cloud apps and other BDC clients, tools and applications.
  3. In SAP Analytics Cloud, create stories, dashboards, and planning models that leverage the Datasphere models for live reporting, seamless planning and simulation.
  4. In SAP HANA Cloud, build high performance end-to-end intelligent applications that leverage custom AI / ML and multi-model capabilities for structured and unstructured data.

This way, the project does not stop at data replication: it delivers an end‑to‑end analytical platform where business users consume trusted, delta‑enabled data products in SAP Business Data Cloud for fact based decision support.

Conclusion

Our pilot project demonstrated that migrating from SAP BW7.5 on HANA to SAP Business Data Cloud using SAP Datasphere is not only feasible but can be done while preserving critical business logic and custom investments.

The key to success lies in thorough planning, understanding the current state of your extraction landscape, and leveraging the migration as an opportunity to modernize while preserving what works. As SAP continues to enhance BDC capabilities, organizations can confidently plan their migration journeys knowing that both standard and custom extraction scenarios are well-supported.

Stay tuned for the 4th blog post in this series, ‘Beyond Data Warehousing, AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1’

entry.title: Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud

 

Modernizing SAP BW is no longer a technical upgrade conversation—it has become a strategic inflection point for enterprises that rely on trusted, governed data but now face a world defined by real‑time decisions, AI‑driven operations, and cloud‑scale innovation. The question leaders are asking has shifted from “Should we modernize BW?” to “How do we evolve BW without disrupting the controls and trust we’ve spent years building?”

SAP’s answer—SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)—signals a fundamental change in how SAP envisions the future of enterprise data. And for BW customers, it represents a rare opportunity: a modernization path that respects the past while unlocking what’s next.

The Pressure on BW Is No Longer Optional

For years, SAP BW has been the backbone of regulated reporting. Its lineage, auditability, and semantic consistency made it indispensable. But the world around it has changed.

The clock is ticking

SAP has extended maintenance for BW 7.5 through 2030 when deployed in Private Cloud Edition, and BW/4HANA remains supported longer. But innovation has clearly moved elsewhere. The runway exists—but it’s finite.

The architecture is showing its age

BW was built for a world where data was centralized, replicated, and processed in batches. Today’s enterprises need something different:

  • Real‑time access to SAP and non‑SAP data
  • Federated architectures that minimize replication
  • AI‑ready data models that can be consumed by Databricks, Snowflake, PowerBI, SAC, and modern analytics tools
  • Elastic cloud scale without the cost of in‑memory sprawl

The gap between what BW was designed for and what enterprises now require is widening.

AI has changed the stakes

AI doesn’t wait for nightly batch loads. It thrives on live, multi‑platform data. And it demands a semantic layer that can be shared across tools—not locked inside a single warehouse.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud enters the picture.

SAP Business Data Cloud: A New Operating Model for SAP Data

BDC is more than a rebranding of Datasphere. It’s SAP’s vision for a unified, governed, cloud‑native data fabric that brings together:

  • SAP BW (via Private Cloud Edition)
  • SAP Datasphere for semantic modeling and federation
  • SAP Databricks for engineering, ML, and AI(depends on the type of license )
  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP‑managed data products and insight applications

The most important shift for BW customers is conceptual: BW objects no longer sit at the center. Business data products do.

 

Instead of converting InfoProviders or CompositeProviders, BW becomes a governed source that exposes its logic as reusable data products—consumable across SAP and non‑SAP platforms. This preserves the trust and lineage BW is known for while enabling modern analytics and AI.

The Modernization Patterns Emerging Across the Market

Enterprises aren’t all taking the same path—and SAP isn’t forcing them to. Three patterns have emerged as the most pragmatic and widely adopted.

1. Lift and Shift BW to Private Cloud Edition

A stability‑first approach that buys time without sacrificing compliance.

  • BW moves “as is” to SAP’s managed private cloud
  • No immediate BW/4HANA conversion
  • BW data becomes available as BDC data products

For regulated industries, this is often the first step—a way to modernize the foundation without rewriting anything.

 

 

2. Hybrid Extension with Datasphere and BDC

Datasphere is the most common pattern for organizations that want innovation without disruption to existing system architecture.

In this approach,

  • BW remains the system of record
  • Selected BW objects are exposed as data products
  • New analytics and AI workloads shift to Datasphere, SAC, and Databricks(if needed)

This approach preserves validated BW logic while enabling cloud‑native capabilities where they matter most.

3. Phased Decommissioning Toward SAP BDC

This option is useful for a long‑term transformation for organizations ready to embrace a cloud‑native future with SAP BDC.

  • Business domains are prioritized
  • Semantics are rebuilt in Datasphere
  • BW flows are gradually retired
  • BDC becomes the enterprise data foundation
 

 

It’s the highest‑effort path—but also the one that positions enterprises for AI‑first architectures.

What Changes When BW Evolves into BDC

The shift from BW to BDC is architectural as much as it is conceptual.

Area

SAP BW

SAP Business Data Cloud

Data Model

InfoCubes, ADSOs

Business data products (CDS View Based)

Storage

Centralized HANA

Object store + federation

Integration

ETL‑heavy

Zero‑copy, delta sharing to external systems

(single point of truth in BDC)

Semantics

BW‑specific

Shared semantic layer

AI Readiness

Limited

SAP AI (Joule, Business AI, GenAI)

 

This is a move from a monolithic warehouse to a federated, semantic, AI‑ready data fabric.

The Business Value Emerging from Early Adopters

Organizations modernizing BW through BDC are seeing tangible benefits:

  • Faster delivery of insights through SAP‑managed data products
  • Lower TCO by shifting from in‑memory persistence to object storage
  • AI enablement without exporting sensitive SAP data
  • A future‑proof architecture without a risky big‑bang migration

The value isn’t theoretical—it’s operational.

Why This Matters for Modernization Leaders

For teams already advising on SAP cloud strategy, BDC becomes the umbrella under which modernization efforts converge. It aligns naturally with:

  • Hybrid analytics strategies
  • Cloud‑first data architectures
  • AI enablement roadmaps
  • Phased modernization approaches

It reframes modernization not as a migration project, but as a strategic evolution.

A Natural Next Step for Enterprises

SAP and partners now offer structured pathways—modernization assessments, discovery workshops, and technical enablement—to help organizations map their BW landscapes to a BDC‑aligned future.

The real opportunity is not simply to modernize BW, but to rethink how SAP data participates in the broader enterprise data ecosystem.

What direction are you leaning toward—stability first, hybrid innovation, or a full cloud‑native future?

entry.title: Efficient Delta Creation in the Datasphere: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
1                Introduction: The Importance of Delta Processing in Modern Data Architectures
Nowadays, large amounts of data are transported from A to B. Therefore, it is becoming increasingly important to either consume or analyze data virtually, or to consume a delta. Since this is not always possible, this article will show how I have solved this problem within the Datasphere.

Step 1: Extracting Data into a Staging Table
The first step is straightforward: extract the data from the source system into a staging table.

At this stage, the goal is not to transform the data but simply to create a 1:1 copy of the source dataset via Replication Flow. This staging layer represents the most recent snapshdot of the source system. 

The staging table acts as the input layer for delta processing. Each load refreshes this table with the latest data extracted from the source.

Step 2: Creating a Hash for Change Detection
To detect changes efficiently, we generate a hash value for each record based on the relevant business attributes.

The idea behind this is simple: If any field changes, the hash value changes as well.

This avoids expensive field-by-field comparisons and keeps the comparison logic compact.

Example for SQL-View for new data:

SELECT
    MATERIAL,
    MATERIAL_DESC,
    QUANTITY,
    HASH_SHA256(
        TO_BINARY(
            MATERIAL || MATERIAL_DESC || QUANTITY
        )
    ) AS HASH_VALUE,
    LOAD_TS
FROM IL_LT_MATERIAL

This hash essentially acts as a fingerprint of the record. When we compare two datasets later, we only need to compare the hash values instead of every column.

For large datasets, this approach can significantly simplify the change detection process.

Beside the new replicated Data we have the "active" Data in another table, which are loaded before. The hash-value is created as well, but over an Transformation Flow. After that we can compare each line over the key (Material).

Step 3: Calculating the Delta
Once the staging data contains the hash values, it can be compared to the current active dataset.

This comparison identifies three possible scenarios:

  • INSERT – the record exists in staging but not in the active dataset
  • UPDATE – the record exists in both datasets but the hash value changed
  • DELETE – the record exists in the active dataset but not in the new staging data

The first time the pipeline runs (initial load), there is no previous dataset to compare against. In that case, the active dataset is simply initialized.

From the second run onward, the system starts identifying real deltas.

Example logic for SQL-View:

SELECT
N.MATERIAL,
N.MATERIAL_DESC,
N.QUANTITY,
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS CHANGE_TS,
CASE
WHEN A.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'I'
WHEN N.HASH_VALUE <> A.HASH_VALUE THEN 'U'
WHEN N.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'DELETE'
END AS DELTA_TYPE
FROM IL_SV_MATERIAL_HASH N
FULL OUTER JOIN IT_LT_MATERIAL_ACTIVE A
ON S.MATERIAL = A.MATERIAL;

Each detected change is stored together with a timestamp, which allows us to track when the change was detected.

Material_DeltaTable

This timestamp becomes particularly useful for auditing, monitoring, and time-based queries.

Step 4: Maintaining the Delta Table
The detected changes are written into a delta table. Instead of storing the full dataset again, this table only contains the records that changed.

Typical columns include:

  • Business key (e.g. MATERIAL, PLANT)
  • Updated values
  • Change type (INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE)
  • Change timestamp

With each new load, additional delta records are appended.

Over time, the table becomes a history of detected changes, which can be extremely helpful for troubleshooting or reconstructing data states.

Step 5: Providing the Delta to Data Consumers
Once the delta table is maintained, it can be exposed to downstream consumers.

Via Replication Flow the Data can be provided to the target, for e.g. ADLS (Premium Outbound neccessary)

Applications, dashboards, or integration services can query the delta table and filter by change type.

For example:

  • retrieve only new records
  • process updates
  • synchronize deletions

Because only the changed data is provided, consumers do not need to process the entire dataset each time.

This keeps integrations lightweight and improves overall system performance.

Process Overview
The complete workflow can be summarized in five steps:

  1. Extract the latest data into a staging table
  2. Generate a hash value for each record
  3. Compare the staging dataset with the active dataset
  4. Identify inserts, updates, and deletes
  5. Store and expose the delta for downstream consumption

entry.title: Using Claude Code and Natural Language to Create SAP Datasphere Artifacts
entry.content:
How It Started

A few weeks ago, I tried an AI tool called Trae_CN to create Datasphere artifacts automatically. After a few hours of testing, it worked well! I was excited and wrote a blog post about it.

AI-Assisted SAP Datasphere Modeling

Then my teams chat filled up with messages from the security team.

The problem? Trae_CN wasn't approved by the company.

So I switched to Claude Code and tried again. This time, I wanted to solve a harder problem: creating complex models automatically. I focused on Analytic Models - the kind that everyone needs but finds hard to automate. These models need many steps: linking tables, setting up measures, and configuring business layers. Usually, you have to do all this by hand in the UI.

If we can use natural language to create these complex models, that would really save time.

I. The Problem: Too Much Manual Work

Data engineers spend a lot of time creating artifacts in SAP Datasphere.

When projects get big:

  • One data warehouse: 50+ tables, 30+ views
  • Complex analytic models: 10+ linked tables

Each artifact needs manual work: open UI → fill in fields → set up links → save

One table takes 5 minutes. 50 tables = 4 hours.

And many of these objects follow the same patterns. They can be automated.

II. The Idea: Use Natural Language

The ideal way should be like this:

"Create a customer table with ID, name, email, and city"
"Create a view from the customer table, show only ID and name"
"Create an analytic model linking customer and product tables"

Just say what you need, and it's done automatically.

III. The Solution: Claude Code + Skills + Datasphere CLI

How It Works

Claude Code understands your words and calls Skills that connect to Datasphere CLI to create artifacts.

The steps:

  1. You describe what you need in plain language
  2. Claude Code understands what you mean
  3. It calls the right Skill
  4. The Skill connects to Datasphere using SAP's official @sap/datasphere-cli tool
  5. It makes the JSON and creates the artifact

An Example

You say:

"Create a customer table with ID, name, and email. ID is the key."

Claude Code runs:

/create-local-table --name CUSTOMER --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,EMAIL:String:100

The Skill creates this JSON:

{
  "definitions": {
    "CUSTOMER": {
      "kind": "entity",
      "elements": {
        "ID": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 10, "key": true },
        "NAME": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 100 },
        "EMAIL": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 100 }
      }
    }
  }
}

Done.

Why Natural Language Works

You can say it different ways:

  • "Make a table with order number, amount, and date"
  • "I need a table for customer information"
  • "Create a sales data table"

Claude Code understands all of these and calls the right Skill.

The Big Challenge: No Format Guide

Problem: Datasphere CLI docs don't show you how to write the CSN format for Replication Flow.

What to do?

Let AI learn it.

The method is simple:

  1. Create an example artifact in Datasphere UI by hand
  2. Use CLI to read that object's full structure
  3. Let Claude Code look at the CSN and find the patterns
  4. Use that template to make new artifacts
# Read an existing artifact
datasphere objects views read --name EXISTING_VIEW

# Claude Code helps figure out:
# - What fields are required?
# - What can we skip?
# - What are the rules?

This "reverse engineering" method helped me learn the right format for all artifact types.

IV. What I Built: 6 Skills, Focus on Analytic Models

The Skills I made:

Skill What It Does

create-local-tableMakes tables
create-viewMakes views
create-analytic-modelMakes analytic models
create-data-flowMakes data flows
create-replication-flowMakes replication flows
create-transformation-flowMakes transformation flows

Analytic Models

Why focus on this one? Because it's the most complex.

Analytic models need:

  • Linking fact and dimension tables
  • Setting up measure calculations
  • Choosing dimension fields
  • Setting up business layers

The old way to create an analytic model:

  1. Drag dimension tables
  2. Set up link fields
  3. Pick dimension fields
  4. Define measures
  5. Save and deploy

Takes 10-20 minutes.

The new way:

/create-view --name SALES_FACT_VW \
  --source SALES_FACT \
  --dimensions CUSTOMER_ID:DIM_CUSTOMER:ID;PRODUCT_ID:DIM_PRODUCT:ID

/create-analytic-model --name AM_SALES \
  --source SALES_FACT_VW \
  --dimensions _CUSTOMER:NAME,CITY;_PRODUCT:NAME,CATEGORY

Done in 30 seconds.

It does this automatically:

  • Links customer dimension (using CUSTOMER_ID)
  • Links product dimension (using PRODUCT_ID)
  • Adds the dimension fields you want (NAME, CITY, CATEGORY)
  • Sets up measure calculations (sum of AMOUNT and QUANTITY)

V. Real Results: Talk in Natural Language

Creating a Sales Analysis Example

You tell Claude Code:

"Create a sales analysis data model for me.

First, make a sales fact table with order number, customer ID, product ID, and amount.

Then make a customer dimension table with ID, name, and city.

Next, make a product dimension table with ID, name, and category.

Then make a fact view based on the sales fact table, linking these two dimensions.

Finally, make an analytic model based on the sales fact view, with amount sum as the measure."

Claude Code does this:

# 1. Create sales fact table
/create-local-table --name SALES_FACT \
  --columns ORDER_ID:String:10:key,CUSTOMER_ID:String:10,PRODUCT_ID:String:10,AMOUNT:Decimal:15:2

# 2. Create customer dimension table
/create-local-table --name DIM_CUSTOMER \
  --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,CITY:String:50 --dimension

# 3. Create product dimension table
/create-local-table --name DIM_PRODUCT \
  --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,CATEGORY:String:50 --dimension

# 4. Create fact view with dimension associations
/create-view --name SALES_FACT_VW \
  --source SALES_FACT \
  --dimensions CUSTOMER_ID:DIM_CUSTOMER:ID;PRODUCT_ID:DIM_PRODUCT:ID

# 5. Create analytic model
/create-analytic-model --name AM_SALES \
  --source SALES_FACT_VW \
  --dimensions _CUSTOMER:NAME,CITY;_PRODUCT:NAME,CATEGORY

What happens:

  • You describe what you need in plain language
  • Claude Code understands and breaks it into steps
  • It calls the right Skills
  • Your whole data model is built

Time to build:

  • Old way: 20-30 minutes
  • Natural language + Claude Code: 1 minute

VI. Open Source Project

The code is on GitHub: https://github.com/yuyonggang/dsp-cli

What's included:

  • 6 complete Skills
  • Full instructions (in English and Chinese)
  • Setup guide
  • Security guide

You can use it as is or change it for your needs.

I built this whole project using Claude Code — AI helped from start to finish.

Final Thoughts

What to Know

1. This Is Still New

Right now, the Skills work best for simple models. For complex business cases:

  • You need to test and try things
  • You might need to fix problems by hand
  • The Skills need more work

This is a step-by-step process, not instant.

2. Be Careful with Big Projects

If your project is complex:

  • Start with simple cases to test
  • Move to harder models slowly
  • Leave time for fixing issues

What's Next

My goal:

Give it a design doc → It creates everything

For example, give an AI Agent tool (like Claude Code) a requirements document:

Sales Analysis System Design
- Sales fact table: order, customer, product, amount, date
- Customer dimension: ID, name, city, country
- Product dimension: ID, name, category, price
- Fact view: based on sales fact table, linking customer and product dimensions
- Analytic model: multi-dimension sales analysis based on fact view

The AI Agent would:

  1. Read the document
  2. Create all tables and dimensions
  3. Build analytic models
  4. Hit an error? Read the error message
  5. Check API docs to understand why
  6. Fix it and try again

Fully automatic, no human help needed.

Using AI Agents to understand needs, work on their own, and fix their own mistakes in Datasphere projects.

entry.title: Triggering SAP Databricks Jobs through SAP Datasphere Task Chains
entry.content:
Abstract

Integrating SAP Datasphere with Databricks opens up powerful possibilities for advanced analytics, machine learning pipelines, and cross-platform data processing.

In this guide, I'll share my experience automating SAP Databricks notebook execution using SAP Datasphere task chains and the Databricks Jobs API through a practical, step-by-step approach.

Common Use Cases:

  • Automated data processing pipelines across SAP and Databricks
  • Machine learning model training triggered by Datasphere data flows
  • Advanced analytics on SAP data using Databricks' compute power
  • Orchestrated cross-platform workflows with unified monitoring

Business Value:

  • Reduced manual intervention in data workflows
  • Faster time-to-insight with automated analytics
  • Unified orchestration across enterprise data platforms

Prerequisites

Before getting started, ensure you have:

  • SAP Datasphere access with permission to create connections and task chains
  • Databricks workspace with notebook development access
  • Databricks Personal Access Token (PAT) generation privileges
  • Basic understanding of REST APIs and HTTP methods
  • A Databricks notebook ready to be executed

Architecture Overview

The integration follows this flow:

┌─────────────────────┐
│  SAP Datasphere     │
│  Task Chain         │
└──────────┬──────────┘
           │
           │ HTTP POST Request
           │ (Jobs API)
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  Databricks         │
│  Jobs API           │
└──────────┬──────────┘
           │
           │ Triggers
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  Databricks         │
│  Notebook Run       │
└─────────────────────┘

Key Components:

  1. Datasphere Task Chain - Orchestration layer
  2. Generic HTTP Connection - API authentication setup
  3. Databricks Jobs API - /api/2.1/jobs/runs/submit endpoint
  4. PAT Authentication - Bearer token security
  5. Databricks Notebook - Target execution logic

Step 1: Create a Generic HTTP Connection in Datasphere

The first step is to establish a secure connection from Datasphere to Databricks. We'll use a Generic HTTP Connection with Bearer token authentication.

Navigate to Connections

In SAP Datasphere, go to Connections and create a new Generic HTTP connection.

Configure Connection Settings

Setting Value Notes

Connection Namedatabricks_rest_apiChoose a descriptive name
Host<your-workspace>.cloud.databricks.comYour Databricks workspace hostname
Port443Datasphere uses 443 (HTTPS) as default
ProtocolHTTPSAlways use secure connections
Path/api/2.1 (optional)Can be set here or in the API task later

Authentication Setup

Databricks uses Bearer tokens (Personal Access Tokens / PATs) for API authentication. For simplicity in Datasphere, we'll configure this as a Username/Password authentication type:

Setting Value

Authentication TypeUsername and Password
Usernametoken
Password<Your Databricks PAT>

Note: The username is literally the string token. This is how Databricks interprets Bearer token authentication via basic auth.

Obtaining a Databricks Personal Access Token (PAT)

You'll need a PAT to authenticate your API calls. Here's how to generate one:

  1. In your Databricks workspace, click on your user profile in the top-right corner
  2. Select Settings
  3. Navigate to Developer under the User section
  4. Click on Manage next to Access Tokens

  1. Click Generate new token
  2. Provide a description (e.g., "Datasphere Task Chain Integration")
  3. Set an expiration period (recommended: 90 days for security)
  4. Copy the generated token immediately as you won't be able to see it again!

Test Your Connection

Before proceeding, verify that your PAT is working correctly by making a test API call:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <your-PAT>" \
  https://<your-workspace>.cloud.databricks.com/api/2.1/jobs/list

If configured correctly, you should receive a JSON response listing your Databricks jobs (or an empty list if you have none as in my case).

Step 2: Create a Datasphere Task Chain

Now that the connection is established, let's create a task chain that will trigger the Databricks notebook.

Create a New Task Chain

  1. In Datasphere, navigate to Data Builder
  2. Create a new Task Chain
  3. Give it a descriptive name (e.g., trigger_databricks_notebook)

Add an API Task

From the task palette, drag the API task onto the canvas.

Step 3: Configure the API Task

This is where we define how Datasphere will call the Databricks Jobs API.

Basic Configuration

Click on the API task to open its configuration panel.

Setting Value

Connectiondatabricks_rest_api (the connection you created earlier)
HTTP MethodPOST

API Endpoint Configuration

The endpoint depends on how you configured your connection:

Setting Value

Full Endpoint (if Path is empty in connection)/api/2.1/jobs/runs/submit
Partial Endpoint (if Path is /api/2.1 in connection)/jobs/runs/submit

This endpoint is used to submit a one-off notebook run without needing a pre-configured Databricks job. For more details, see the Databricks Jobs API documentation.

Request Body

The request body defines what notebook to run and with what parameters. Here's what I used:

{
    "name": "<job-name>",
    "tasks": [{
        "task_key": "run_notebook",
        "notebook_task": {
            "notebook_path": "/Workspace/Users/<your-user>/<your-notebook>"
        },
        "environment_key": "Default",
        "timeout_seconds": 3600
    }],
    "environments": [{
        "environment_key": "Default",
        "spec": {
            "client": "1"
        }
    }],
    "max_concurrent_runs": 1
}

Request Body Breakdown

Field Purpose

nameA descriptive name for this job run (appears in Databricks UI)
tasks[].task_keyUnique identifier for this task within the job
tasks[].notebook_task.notebook_pathFull path to your Databricks notebook
tasks[].environment_keyReferences the environment configuration
tasks[].timeout_secondsMaximum execution time (3600 = 1 hour)
environments[].environment_keyEnvironment identifier (must match task's reference)
environments[].spec.clientClient specification for the environment
max_concurrent_runsHow many instances of this job can run simultaneously

Serverless Compute Note: The example above uses Databricks Serverless Compute, so no cluster specification is required. If you're using a dedicated cluster, you'll need to add a new_cluster or existing_cluster_id field in the task definition.

Response Configuration

For monitoring purposes, configure the response handling:

Setting Value

Response TypeHTTP Status Code
Success Status Codes200, 201

The API will return a 200 OK status if the notebook run was successfully triggered, along with a JSON response containing the run_id.

 

Step 4: Test Your Task Chain

Before deploying to production, thoroughly test your integration.

Execute the Task Chain

  1. Save your task chain configuration
  2. Click Run to execute the task chain manually
  3. Monitor the execution in the Datasphere task chain log

Verify in Databricks

  1. Navigate to your Databricks workspace
  2. Go to Jobs & Pipelines
  3. You should see a new run with the name datasphere_job (or whatever you specified)
  4. Click on the run to see execution details and logs

entry.title: Pssst! Want to learn a Datasphere secret?
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello, and welcome to yet another entry in my ongoing hit series (which I really should come up with a name for at some point), where I  try to close the gab between the old BW and Datasphere, or just try to help me fellow developers get to grips with life in Datasphere. 

Today, I'll use an edge-case to teach you some fundamental tricks that you can use in Datasphere.
I'll show you how to implement language-independent texts in a Text view, even though SAP does not want me to. 
See note 3221466 for reference.

A bit of background

So, if you've been playing around with SAP Datasphere , you'll have come across the framework of creating Dimension views and Text views, and linking them together through a Text-Association, which enables reporting on both IDs and Descriptions, in multiple languages. 

This method of creating a data model should be very familiar to people who've done CDS development, where you do the exact same thing. 
For BW developers, it may seem a bit odd, but BW does the exact same thing when you enable texts for an InfoObject. It creates a separate text table, regardless of whether or not you specify language-dependence or not. 

The problem

In Datasphere however, if you create a text view and you do not specify a language column, you'll get an error. 

In note 3221466, SAP specifies that this is the intended behavior. 
If you want a text without a language, that is, language independent texts, you have to add that column directly to the dimension, like below:

Wait, what's the problem?

Yes, so you may (correctly) raise an eyebrow and ask why on earth I keep getting upset over little things like this. 

Similar to my other blog post about translation texts, it may seem like I'm overreacting slightly. But this problem, just like the other one, has been a solved problem for decades in SAP software. 

So, dear reader, It bothers me to no end, because of three things:

  1. When working with CDS views, you absolutely can omit a language column in a text entity, and it works just fine. 
  2. What if you suddenly have to support multiple languages? Now you have to go through every single dimension and create new views, new associations and so on, rather than just being allowed to build it 'correctly' in the first place. 

But, the third and most painful, is that there is a very easy way to fix it. 

So, what can we do?

Before I go into the solution, I would like to take a moment out to complain a bit about the design of the Datasphere, that grates a bit on me. 

The problem deserves a longer blog at some point, but the short version is: Datasphere (or HANA Cloud, which Datasphere runs on) can do a lot more than the Datasphere documentation lets on.
At best I would say that it's though to write proper documentation, so "easter eggs" are to be expected. But at worst, I would say the following is going to be as much a surprise to the Datasphere developers as it is to you. 

Let's hope that's not the case. That would be laughable. 

The solution to our problem a little thing called sessions variables, which are some metadata values to your session.

You may actually have hit upon the SESSION_CONTEXT function when creating a view:

If you go to the official documentation you'll be served a rather cryptic message:

The <session_variable> can either be predefined or user-defined. Predefined session variables that can be set by the client are APPLICATION, APPLICATIONUSER, and TRACEPROFILE.

Which may give you the impression that those are the three options. But that is a lie. A big fat, stupid lie. 

If you have access to a database user (follow the steps in this blog post), you can 

select * from SYS.M_SESSION_CONTEXT	where CONNECTION_ID = CURRENT_CONNECTION;

You'll get something like this:

Which is the session variables for the HANA Explorer session.
But you can do something funny:

select distinct "KEY" from SYS.M_SESSION_CONTEXT;

Which will give you all the possible session variables, across all users. Here is a subset:

KEY
APPLICATION
APPLICATIONSTACK
APPLICATIONUSER
APPLICATIONVERSION
DRIVERVERSION
LOCALE
LOCALE_SAP
PROTOCOL_VERSION
SAP_PASSPORT
SPATIAL_OUTPUT_REPRESENTATION
XS_APPLICATIONUSER
XS_CLIENT_ID
XS_CUSTOM1
XS_CUSTOM2
XS_CUSTOM3
XS_CUSTOM4
XS_CUSTOM5
XS_DISPLAYNAME
XS_EMAIL
XS_FAMILYNAME

Which means, in your SQL console, you can try something like:

select *, SESSION_CONTEXT('LOCALE') from SYS.M_SESSION_CONTEXT where connection_id = CURRENT_CONNECTION;

And you'll get something like this:

Back in Datasphere, you can create an SQL view, you can use the "CURRECT_CONNECTION" function to get the session ID. This session is different than the HANA Explorer above:

"But Christian, do I have to use an SQL view? Can't I use a graphical view?"  You may reasonable ask.
