No. When you create a graphical view, some validation checks against a set og legal statements. 
In an SQL view, you can use almost all the HANA SQL functions. A lot of fun stuff in there, like JSON-query.

"Okay... Why? That seems incredibly arbitrary"  You ask. Again reasonably.

And you know what - stop asking so many questions, reader! I'm having a meltdown here. Why does it always have to be about you?

Anyway.

I've provided some sample values for a session when you press "Data Preview" in a view.

KEYVALUE
APPLICATIONdragonet
APPLICATIONUSER<some value>
XS_APPLICATIONUSER<some value>
DRIVERVERSION02.27.23
PROTOCOL_VERSION4.1 (6,  10)
SAP_PASSPORT<some value>
XS_CLIENT_IDsb-approuter-sac-saceu20!<value>
XS_CUSTOM1[]
XS_CUSTOM2[]
XS_CUSTOM3[]
XS_CUSTOM4[]
XS_CUSTOM5[]
XS_DISPLAYNAME["Christian Gravesen"]
XS_EMAIL<some value>
XS_FAMILYNAME["Gravesen"]
XS_FAMILY_NAME<some value>
XS_FUNCTIONALAREA[]
XS_GIVENNAME["Christian"]
XS_GIVEN_NAME<some value>
XS_GROUPS["sac"]
XS_ISS<some value>
XS_PREFERREDLANGUAGE[]
XS_USER_NAME<some value>
XS_ZID<some value>

The XS_ variables are all related to your SAML / Identity provider properties. 

Notice something? The "LOCALE" and "LOCALE_SAP" is gone. 
But does that mean you can't use 'SESSION_CONTEXT('LOCALE')' in Datasphere views?

No! You absolutely can, even though it isn't in the list. Look!!!

And the output will be the value not match you chosen language - obviously. 
But! If you use that view in an analytical model (or a SAC story, or through the Excel plugin), it will match your "Data Access Language". 

"Well Christian, how the hell was I supposed to know that?"  You ask. 

You weren't! You have to be a bleeding idiot, or just clinically insane, to keep banging your head against the wall like I've done to figure these things out. 
But that's why you have me to do that. Lucky you. 

The actual solution now, promise.

Okay, so at this point, you might have already guessed the solution. 

You can create your Dimension view and your text view. If you don't have a "language" column yet, you can add a calculated column, 

left(SESSION_CONTEXT('LOCALE'),5)

and your language value will always match whatever the user is requesting. 
Note: The semantics for the language column is String(5), and can contain one to five characters. Everything form the old one-letter SAP locales (E for English, D for German, etc.), up to the modern ISO 3166 language / country formats (en_UK for English, en_US for Simplified English, etc.)

And that's it! Solved! 
You can create your text views for all your dimensions, whether or not they have a language column (yet), build your dimensions, build your models, and everything will work just perfect. 

"But Christian, why do I have to do that? Couldn't SAP do that for a text view in some runtime magic, if I don't define a language column?" 

Yes. That's how CDS views work. It's really that simple. 
But, you may ask, why the hell does Datasphere not work that way? Surely there is a complex, logical, highly technical reason?

And yes, dear reader. If we give people the benefit of the doubt, sure - there may be good reasons. 
But alas, dear reader, my guess is no. I'm beginning to suspect that whoever is developing Datasphere has never stopped to ask:

"Wait, this dosen't work. Maybe, maybe in our 50 years of software development, someone else already figured this out? Maybe I'll ask around the office". 

Either that, or not a single senior developer is on the same planet as the development team. 
But that would be absolutely insane. So surely that is not the case. 

And listen - I know we can't expect all the same functionality the old BW systems offered. That thing was developed over 20 years - I totally accept that. A gab in functionality - fine. 

But these are not specific problems - they are general problems across SAP software that was already solved countless times. Maybe, maybe we should expect that at least those problems are not reintroduced. 

Anyway, that's the post. 

I still like Datasphere, by the way. It's pretty good, all things considered.

entry.title: The Next Era of Business Data Webinar Series
entry.content:
Join the Next Era of Business Data webinar series to hear directly from SAP experts and learn how BDC helps data professionals unify, govern, and activate SAP and non-SAP data for enabling streamlined analytics, governance, and AI-driven insights. This series is perfect for data engineers, analysts, architects, and anyone shaping a modern SAP data strategy.

Discover recorded and upcoming sessions and register: https://events.sap.com/data-analytics/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Automated SAP Datasphere TaskChains with SAP CLI, Batch and Windows Scheduler
entry.content:
Introduction

In large SAP landscapes, Task Chains often run repeatedly and require monitoring. Manual supervision can therefore be time- and cost-consuming. By combining the SAP CLI, batch scripting, and Windows Task Scheduler, you can fully automate Task Chain execution, monitoring, and error handling.

Use Case: This automation approach is particularly suitable for Task Chains that may fail due to missing responses from source systems or because of long runtime/response times. In these cases, a simple automatic restart can resolve the issue. This method is not recommended for all Task Chains, as persistent errors may indicate a deeper problem that cannot be resolved by a restart.

Before continuing with the step-by-step guide on how to set up your own automated SAP Datasphere Task Chain, I will briefly describe the architecture of the automated SAP Datasphere Task Chain process.

Archtiecture Overview

The Windows Server hosts the Task Scheduler and the Batch Script. We need the the Task Scheduler to execute the Batch Script at configured intervals. The Batch Script contains logic to check the Task Chain status, writes logs and restart the TaskChain if needed. With the SAP Datasphere Command Line Interface (CLI) we have the possibility to acces our TaskChains via OAuth authentifaction. SAP Datasphere CLI acts as an interface between the Batch Script and SAP Datasphere Task Chain.

With this approach the TaskChains in SAP Datasphere are executed and monitored automatically.

Step-by-Step Guide

Install SAP Datasphere CLI

To control your Task Chain over the CLI the following prerequisites should be fulfilled:

  1. Install Node.js on Windows  ( https://nodejs.org/en/download/ )
  2. Install SAP Datapshere CLI globally with the following command

                            npm install -g @sap/datasphere-cli

      3. To check if the installation was sucessful, run:

                            datasphere --version

Make sure, that Node.js is correctly in your System Path , so the CLI Command is recognized.

Connect CLI to Datapshere Tenant

Configure CLI and log on to your SAP Datasphere Tenant  

datasphere config host set https://yourdataspheretenant.eu10.hcs.cloud.sap

datasphere login

 

To log into the Command Line Interface a OAuth2.0 Client with an interactive usage purpose is required. If you have an administrator role you can create OAuth2.0 Client by yourself. Follow the instructions in the SAP Help Portal "Create an OAuth2.0 Client with an Interactive Usage Purpose | SAP Help Portal"

After creating the client, log in with your Client ID and Secret. If the login is successful a confirmation window will appear. Afterwards you won't need to repeat the log in for 720 hours.

 

 

 

Test Task Chain Commands

Below are some commands you can test. Make sure they return the excpected results before proceeding with the automation of the Task Chain.

  1. Call the Stauts of a Task Chain:

              datasphere tasks logs get --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --log-id 10183

       2. List all Logs of a Task Chain:

            datasphere tasks logs list --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --objectname DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

      3. Execute a Task Chain manually:

            datasphere tasks chains run --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --object DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

      4. You can list all available commands with the follwing command

            datasphere --h

Create a Batch File

After setting up the conection to SAP Datasphere through SAP CLI we will create a Batch Script not only to automate and monitor our task chains but also to restart task chains if the status is FAILED.

You can use the following script as a template:

@echooff

setlocal enabledelayedexpansion

set SPACE_ID=technical_name_of_your_space_ID

set TASKCHAIN_NAME=DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

set LOGFILE=C:\Users\...\Logs\taskchain_watch.log

 

if not exist "C:\Users\...\Logs" (

    mkdir "C:\Users\...\Logs"

)

 

REM determine last log ID

for /f "tokens=2 delims=:" %%a in ('

  datasphere tasks logs list --space %SPACE_ID% --objectname %TASKCHAIN_NAME% ^| findstr /i "logId"

') do (

  set LOG_ID=%%a

  set LOG_ID=!LOG_ID:,=!

  goto GET_STATUS

)

 

REM Check Status of Task Chain

:GET_STATUS

for /f %%a in ('

  datasphere tasks logs get --space %SPACE_ID% --log-id %LOG_ID%

') do (

  set STATUS=%%a

)

 

set STATUS=!STATUS:"=!

 

REM Neustart bei FAILED

if /i "!STATUS!"=="FAILED" (

    datasphere tasks chains run --space %SPACE_ID% --object %TASKCHAIN_NAME%

    echo %date% %time% FAILED erkannt – Task Chain neu gestartet >> "%LOGFILE%"

    goto :EOF

)

 

echo %date% %time% Status OK (!STATUS!) >> "%LOGFILE%"

 

Explanation:

  • @echooff  - suppresses display of commands
  • set -  definition of variables
  • for -  processing outputs in loops
  • if -  performs conditional checks
  • goto -  jumps to a defined script marker

Automate with Windows Task Scheduler

Now we move on to the automation part.

  1. Open Task Scheduler and click on "Create Task"
  2. On the General Tab give a name and Description  and also check "Only run, if usesr is logged" button

      3. On the Trigger Tab set up your individual Schedule (daily, weekly, monthly, etc.)

      4. On the Actions tab select your batch script 

After setting up your scheduled task make sure that logs are generated and the Task Chain restart by the Status FAILED.

 

 

Conclusion/Summary

By combining SAP CLI, Batch Script, and Windows Task Scheduler, you can now execute and monitor Task Chains automatically. Failures are detected and resolved by restarting the Task Chain, and logs are generated for traceability.

Optional enhancements:

  • Add email notifications for FAILED Task Chains
  • Monitor multiple Task Chains simultaneously
  • Centralize logs for team access
  • Limit the number of automatic restarts (e.g., max 3 retries) to identify persistent issues that require manual intervention

entry.title: Administering Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
Why Administration Matters

Before organizations can effectively manage sustainability data, they need a solid technical foundation. SAP Sustainability Control Tower requires proper setup, role management, and system integration to ensure accurate, consistent, and scalable operations.

A well-administered system enables:

  • Reliable data integration across SAP landscapes
  • Secure and structured user access
  • Smooth operation for business users
  • A strong foundation for reporting and steering sustainability

What You’ll Learn in This Learning Journey

This compact, 1.5-hour course focuses on the essential administrative tasks required to get SAP Sustainability Control Tower up and running:

  1. Setting Up Accounts and Services
    Learn how to manage accounts and services to prepare the system for use.
  2. Assigning Roles
    Understand different user roles and how to assign them to ensure proper access control and governance.
  3. Configuring System Integration
    Connect SAP Sustainability Control Tower with key systems, including:
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP EHS Management
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Analytics Cloud

This ensures seamless data exchange and a fully connected sustainability landscape.

Prerequisite: Basic knowledge from the Learning Journey Reporting and Steering Sustainability in Your Businesses using SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is designed for:

  • System Administrators responsible for setup and configuration

Build the technical foundation for effective sustainability management and start the Learning Journey “Administering SAP Sustainability Control Tower” here.

entry.title: The Data Ownership dilemma — and how SAP Business Data Cloud Data Products helps resolve It
entry.content:
The core problem: Data belongs to the Business, but IT manages it

Many enterprises agree in principle that “data is a business asset.” The business generates the data, uses it daily, and relies on it for decision‑making. Yet, in practice, IT manages most of the systems, pipelines, and storage, creating a structural contradiction:

  • Business claims ownership, but
  • IT controls the handling.

This leads to a classic accountability gap:
Business expects IT to provide clean, reliable, well-modeled data, while IT expects business units to define rules, definitions, and quality expectations. The misunderstanding is structural.

When data is copied Into a Data Platform, the gap widens

As soon as data is replicated into a platform such as a data lake, data warehouse, the conversation changes:

  • The business often assumes that data copied into the platform becomes IT’s responsibility entirely.
  • IT, however, needs business input to validate transformations, definitions, mappings, and quality checks.

The outcome is predictable:

  1. IT publishes the replicated data, structuring it according to their best understanding.
  2. Business reviews it and complains:
    • “This data is not clean.”
    • “These definitions don’t match our business reality.”
    • “The numbers don’t reconcile with the source data.”

This friction exists because ownership is unclear at every stage: raw data, transformed data, curated views, and analytic models.

The hidden trap: Recreating an ERP inside a Data Platform

One of the most common pitfalls in this context is that all ERP intelligence ends -up being recreated in the data platform.
Why does this happen?
When business users review data in the data platform, they often compare results with ERP transactions. Any deviation is seen as an error. This pushes IT teams to:

  • Rebuild ERP logic,
  • Recreate document flows,
  • Mirror every table, relationship, and constraint,
  • Re‑implement ERP-specific business rules.

Recreating ERP logic leads to:

  • Massive data model complexity
  • Fragile pipelines that break when ERP updates occur
  • Endless reconciliation loops
  • Slow delivery cycles
  • High cost and low return

Data ownership must evolve — and the SAP SAP Business Data Cloud platform enable that evolution

The tension between business and IT will not disappear simply with new tooling. But SAP’s Business Data Cloud introduces capabilities that directly address the root causes:

Within SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP provides out-of-the-box, ready to consume predefined data flows and models based on every SAP applications : Data Products. These Data Products carry business semantics, and application context. They are perfectly aligned with the business definition of the data and ready to consume.

That means:

  • Business has access to the very same data than in their applications.
  • Data definitions are explicit.
  • IT handles the platform; business handles meaning.
  • Conflicts reduce because expectations are defined in the product lifecycle, supervised by SAP.

By providing curated, governed Data Products sourced directly from SAP applications, Data Products expose predefined structures and metadata, meaning you’re consuming business-ready, not system-ready, data.

This is how BDC helps addressing the data ownership problem :

Problem

Impact

How SAP BDC Helps

Business claims ownership but delegates responsibility to IT

Misunderstandings, poor quality feedback

Data Products enforce decentralized, domain-based ownership with structured metadata and lifecycle governance

Copying data creates ambiguity over who validates it

IT guesses business logic; business rejects results

Data Products include business semantics and expose data via standard interfaces that business can validate early

Recreating ERP logic in the data platform

Complex, costly, brittle architectures

BDC offers curated, SAP-native data products designed for analytics and AI without reconstructing operational logic

Conclusion: Data ownership is a shared responsibility — with the right tools

Data ownership cannot sit solely with IT or solely with the business. It must be shared and formalized.

SAP SAP Business Data Cloud , through its standardized Data Products, provides a practical way to bridge the gap:

  • Business defines semantics, meaning, and rules.
  • IT manages infrastructure, governance frameworks, and integration.
  • Both collaborate on iterative product lifecycles rather than one‑off pipeline deliveries.

Organizations adopting this model move from blame cycles to collaborative stewardship, unlocking faster delivery, higher trust, and cleaner data.

entry.title: Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere: The Next Level
entry.content:
Most currency conversion setups in SAP Datasphere stop at “it’s working.” But what if you could make them smarter, more flexible, and easier to maintain, without complicating your model?

In this blog, we take the standard currency conversion in SAP Datasphere and push it a step further. Building on the traditional setup (for the basics, see the following blog), we look at a common finance scenario: a user wants to view net prices either in their original transaction currency or in a specific currency they choose through an input parameter. This is typically handled using the standard currency conversion, configured with an input parameter for the target currency and set to keep unconverted values when errors occur.

However, this setup leads to an important inconsistency. Before conversion, the amount is treated as a monetary field. After conversion, SAP classifies it as a regular measure. As a result, the converted value is aggregated like any other numeric field—potentially misleading users who assume the total represents a single currency. Meanwhile, the original monetary field (in this case NetPriceAmount) behaves correctly: SAP recognizes it as multi‑currency and displays the * symbol instead of aggregating it into a single value (like done at the Standard_Currency_Conversion field).

The challenge is that SAP’s standard currency conversion doesn’t allow us to use an input parameter as the unit column (only other fields), which prevents the converted measure from being recognized as a true currency amount.

With the improved Currency Conversion, this behavior changes entirely. Instead of relying directly on the input parameter, we populate a dedicated column with the selected currency and assign that column as the Unit Column for the converted measure. By giving this measure the semantic type “Amount with Currency,” SAP now treats it as a true monetary field. The converted value then behaves just like the original monetary column (such as NetPriceAmount), ensuring consistent aggregation rules and a clearer reporting experience.

 

With this improved setup, you get consistent semantics, cleaner aggregation, and a more intuitive reporting experience. While this approach works in both graphical views and analytical models, we’ll focus on the analytical model, as it offers the fastest and most efficient implementation. More on that later in the blog.

How-To Instructions

Phase 1: Configure the Graphical View

To begin, you must establish a base fact view that handles the logic, target currency attribute and input parameter, for your target currency.

1. Create the View:

Create the view containing the fields requiring conversion.

2. Define Input Parameter(s):

Create an input parameter (e.g., TARGET_CURRENCY) to receive the user's desired currency.

Important: Assign a default value like NO_CONV. This allows the model to differentiate between a request for conversion and keeping the original currency.

3. Create a Target Currency Attribute:

Add a calculated column (e.g., TargetCurrency_c). In this column, use logic to ensure that if the input is the default NO_CONV, the column reflects the source currency; otherwise, it takes the value from the input parameter.

4. Set Semantic Type:

Change the semantic type of this new column to Currency Code to ensure the system recognizes it during aggregation.

Phase 2: Implementation in the Analytical Model

While this logic works in graphical views, using an Analytical Model is recommended for significantly better performance—completing queries in approximately 1 second compared to 6 seconds in a graphical view (according to my measurements).

1. Generate the Model:

Create a new Analytical Model based on the graphical view configured in Phase 1. Attributes and parameters will inherit automatically.

2. Add a Conversion Measure:

Under the Measures section, select the + icon and choose Conversion Measure.

 

3. Configure Settings:

Important settings:

  1. Target Currency: Reference the TargetCurrency_c attribute column created earlier (Phase 1 – Step 3).
  2. Error Handling: Set this to Fail on Error for robust data integrity.
  3. Reference Date: Ensure a date column (like SalesDocumentDate) is selected to pull the correct exchange rates.

 For more details on configuration of the currency conversion, I refer to this SAP blog on currency conversion.

Phase 3: Validation and Testing

Use the Preview function to confirm the model behaves as expected under different scenarios:

Scenario A (Default):
Leave the parameter as NO_CONV. The model should display values in their original source currencies.

Scenario B (Conversion):
Enter a specific currency code (e.g., USD). The model should apply the conversion logic and display all values in the target currency.

Conclusion

By taking this small architectural step, adding a dynamic unit column and letting the Analytical Model do the heavy lifting, you turn currency conversion in Datasphere into something far more reliable and intuitive. Your money oriented measures keep their behavior, your totals stay honest, and your users finally get a consistent experience whether they convert or not.

If you’ve tried this approach or have questions about adapting it to your own scenario, feel free to drop a comment. I’m always curious to hear how others tackle multi‑currency challenges.

entry.title: My 26-Year BI Semantic Layer Journey: From BusinessObjects to SAP's Cloud & AI Future
entry.content:
When I first joined Business Objects back in 1998, the world of Business Intelligence was a fundamentally different landscape. Data lived on-premise. Servers hummed in corporate basements. The "cloud" was something you saw out the window, not something you deployed a data model on. Over the past 26 years, I've had a front-row seat — and often a hands-on role — in the evolution of the BI semantic layer, from its earliest incarnations as a simple metadata abstraction layer to today's AI-infused, cloud-native intelligent data platforms.

This blog is a reflection on that journey: the technologies I've worked with, the strategic pivots I've witnessed, and where SAP's current vision is taking us next.

1998–2008: The Foundation Years — UNV & BEx Queries (On-Premise)

Business Objects Universe (.UNV)

My journey began in 1998 when I joined Business Objects, a company that was arguably the pioneer of the modern BI semantic layer concept. At the heart of everything was the Universe (.UNV) — a metadata layer that abstracted the complexity of relational databases into business-friendly objects that end users could drag and drop to build queries and reports.

The Universe Designer was elegant in its purpose: it sat between the raw database schema and the business user, translating SQL joins, complex table structures, and database-specific syntax into intuitive classes, objects, and predefined filters. Business users didn't need to know SQL. They didn't need to understand star schemas or snowflake models. They simply needed to understand their business questions, and the Universe would handle the rest.

This was a purely on-premise world. The Universe connected to on-premise relational databases — Oracle, SQL Server, DB2, Teradata — and the entire BI stack, from the Connection Server to the Central Management Server (CMS) to the Web Intelligence report server, lived behind the corporate firewall. Deployment was complex. Upgrades were monumental. But the semantic layer concept was powerful, and it became the backbone of enterprise reporting for thousands of organizations worldwide.

SAP BW & BEx Queries

On the SAP side of the house, SAP Business Warehouse (BW) was the dominant analytics platform, and its semantic layer equivalent was the BEx Query. Built on top of BW InfoProviders — InfoCubes, DSOs, MultiProviders — BEx Queries defined the analytical view of data using key figures, characteristics, variables, and calculated/restricted key figures.

BEx Query Designer was the tool of choice for SAP BW developers, and BEx Analyzer (an Excel add-in) was the primary consumption tool for business users. The BEx query served as the semantic definition layer that determined what data was exposed, how it was filtered, and how it was presented.

For organizations running SAP ERP, BW with BEx was the standard analytics path. The data pipeline was clear: ERP → BW (via extractors and data flows) → BEx Query → BEx Analyzer or Web Application Designer.

Key takeaway from this era: The semantic layer was established as an essential architectural component. Whether you were in the Business Objects ecosystem or the SAP BW ecosystem, the principle was the same — abstract complexity, empower business users, govern data access. But everything was on-premise, and the two ecosystems existed largely in parallel.

2010: The Convergence — UNX, Multisource Universes & the Dawn of HANA

SAP's Acquisition of Business Objects (2007) and Its Aftermath

SAP's acquisition of Business Objects in 2007 was transformative. By 2010, we were deep into the work of integrating two powerful but distinct BI ecosystems. One of the most significant technical outcomes was the introduction of the UNX Universe — the next-generation semantic layer built in the new Information Design Tool (IDT).

UNX — The Multisource Universe

The .UNX format was more than a file format upgrade from .UNV. It represented a fundamental architectural shift. The multisource-enabled Universe introduced the concept of a Data Foundation that could federate data from multiple heterogeneous sources within a single semantic layer. You could join tables from an Oracle database with tables from a SQL Server database — or even with OLAP sources — within one Universe.

This was significant because it acknowledged an emerging reality: enterprise data was no longer confined to a single monolithic database. Organizations were beginning to experiment with cloud-based data sources, SaaS application data, and Hadoop-based data lakes. The multisource Universe, powered by the Data Federation engine, was SAP's answer to this growing complexity.

The Information Design Tool also introduced:

  • Business Layer separation from the Data Foundation, enabling cleaner semantic modeling
  • Linked Universes and shared Data Foundations for reuse and governance
  • Support for OLAP connections (including BW via BICS) alongside relational connections
  • Enhanced security modeling with business security profiles and data security profiles

The UNX Universe could now serve as a unified semantic layer across on-premise and early cloud data sources, marking the beginning of the hybrid era.

SAP HANA — Calculation Views & HANA CDS Views

2010 also marked the emergence of SAP HANA — a game-changing in-memory database and application platform that would reshape SAP's entire product strategy.

From a semantic layer perspective, HANA introduced its own modeling constructs:

  • Calculation Views — the primary HANA native semantic layer, created in HANA Studio (an Eclipse-based IDE). Calculation Views allowed developers to build complex analytical models using graphical or scripted (SQL Script) approaches, incorporating joins, unions, aggregations, calculated columns, input parameters, and hierarchies directly within the database layer.
  • HANA CDS Views — Core Data Services views that brought a higher-level, annotation-rich modeling language to HANA, enabling semantically rich data models with associations, annotations for analytics, and built-in authorization concepts.

The significance of HANA's semantic layer was profound: it pushed the semantic modeling into the database itself. Rather than having a separate metadata layer sitting on top of the database (as with UNV/UNX) or a separate query layer (as with BEx), HANA's Calculation Views meant that the database was simultaneously the computation engine and the semantic definition layer. This "code-to-data" paradigm eliminated data movement, leveraged in-memory performance, and enabled real-time analytics on transactional data.

Key takeaway from this era: The semantic layer was no longer a single-technology concept. It was becoming a multi-tier, federated architecture — Universes for cross-source abstraction, HANA Calculation Views for in-database modeling, BEx Queries for BW-based analytics — all coexisting within the SAP ecosystem. The hybrid data landscape was emerging, and SAP's semantic layer strategy was evolving to meet it.

2015–2026: The Cloud Transformation — From BW/4HANA to Business Data Cloud

This decade-plus period represents the most dramatic acceleration in the evolution of SAP's BI semantic layer and overall analytics strategy. Each year brought new platforms, new tools, and new paradigms — all moving inexorably toward the cloud.

BW/4HANA — BW Modeling Tool

SAP BW/4HANA, launched in 2016, was the cloud-optimized, HANA-native evolution of SAP BW. Gone were legacy BW objects like InfoCubes and DSOs, replaced by a simplified, HANA-optimized data model based on:

  • Advanced DSOs (aDSOs) — a single, flexible storage object
  • CompositeProviders — for virtual federation and union of data sources
  • Open ODS Views — for virtualizing external HANA tables and views
  • HANA Calculation View integration — seamless embedding of HANA native models

The BW Modeling Tool (Eclipse-based) became the primary development environment, replacing the legacy BW workbench (RSA1). BEx Queries continued to serve as the semantic consumption layer, but now they could sit on top of HANA-optimized BW models that leveraged HANA's in-memory engine for pushdown execution.

BW/4HANA represented SAP's strategy of modernizing the on-premise data warehouse while preparing customers for eventual cloud migration. It was "cloud-ready" in architecture but still deployed on-premise or in private cloud environments.

S/4HANA — Embedded Analytics & CDS Views

SAP S/4HANA brought another paradigm shift — embedding analytics directly into the transactional application. The semantic layer here was built on ABAP CDS Views with rich annotations that defined:

  • Analytical queries and cubes
  • Associations and joins
  • Authorization checks
  • UI consumption metadata (for Fiori analytical apps)

S/4HANA Embedded Analytics eliminated the traditional "extract, load, and report" pattern by enabling real-time analytical queries directly on the transactional database. The CDS view became the semantic layer for both transactional and analytical consumption, blurring the line between OLTP and OLAP in ways that were previously impossible.

For BI professionals, this meant that the semantic layer was now embedded in the application layer — a significant departure from the traditional pattern of separate BI semantic layers sitting on top of replicated data.

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Import & Live Connection Models

SAP Analytics Cloud represented SAP's flagship cloud-native analytics platform, and it introduced its own semantic constructs:

  • Import Models — data is ingested into SAC's in-memory store (powered by HANA Cloud under the hood). The model definition — dimensions, measures, hierarchies, formulas — is defined within SAC itself. This is a self-contained cloud semantic layer.
  • Live Connection Models — SAC connects in real-time to backend sources (BW, BW/4HANA, S/4HANA, HANA) and leverages the backend semantic layer (BEx Queries, HANA Calculation Views, CDS Views) for data definition. SAC in this mode is a visualization and planning front-end, not a semantic layer itself.

The live vs. import duality reflected the hybrid reality of SAP's customer base: many organizations still had critical data on-premise and needed real-time access, while others were ready to move data to the cloud for self-service analytics.

SAC also introduced planning models, predictive capabilities, and smart features (Smart Insights, Smart Discovery) — early signals of the AI direction that would become central to SAP's strategy.

SAP HANA Cloud — Business Application Studio

As HANA moved to the cloud, so did its semantic modeling capabilities. SAP HANA Cloud brought Calculation Views to a cloud-native deployment, and the development environment shifted from HANA Studio to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) — a cloud-based IDE built on Eclipse Theia.

Developing Calculation Views in BAS meant that the entire development lifecycle — modeling, testing, deploying — could happen in the cloud. This was significant for organizations adopting SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as their innovation platform, enabling cloud-native data modeling alongside application development, integration, and AI/ML services.

HANA Cloud also introduced enhanced data federation and replication capabilities, allowing Calculation Views to virtualize data from on-premise HANA, third-party databases, and cloud data sources — extending the semantic layer across hybrid landscapes.

SAP Datasphere — Graphical Views & the Business Semantic Layer

SAP Datasphere (formerly SAP Data Warehouse Cloud) represents perhaps the most significant evolution of the BI semantic layer in SAP's cloud portfolio. Datasphere was purpose-built to be the unified data integration and semantic layer for SAP's cloud analytics strategy.

Key semantic layer capabilities include:

  • Graphical Views — a visual, no-code/low-code modeling environment for building data transformations, joins, unions, calculated columns, and aggregations. Graphical Views are accessible to a broader audience than traditional Calculation Views, democratizing semantic modeling.
  • Analytical Datasets and Dimensions — purpose-built semantic types that define how data is exposed for analytical consumption
  • Business Layer — a business-friendly semantic abstraction that maps technical data models to business terminology, complete with associations, hierarchies, and authorization
  • Spaces — governance constructs that manage data access, resource allocation, and collaboration boundaries

Datasphere's semantic layer is designed to federate and harmonize data from across the hybrid landscape — SAP systems (S/4HANA, BW, SuccessFactors, Ariba), non-SAP databases, cloud data lakes, and third-party applications. It serves as the single point of semantic truth for SAP Analytics Cloud and other consumption tools.

The integration between Datasphere and SAC is particularly tight: Datasphere models are directly consumable in SAC via live connections, creating a seamless model → visualize → plan → act workflow.

SAP Databricks — Custom AI/ML

SAP's partnership with Databricks represents the convergence of BI and AI/ML. Through this collaboration, organizations can:

  • Leverage Databricks' Lakehouse architecture for large-scale data processing
  • Build custom AI and Machine Learning models using Databricks' ML runtime, MLflow, and collaborative notebooks
  • Integrate Databricks-processed data and ML model outputs back into SAP Datasphere and SAC for business consumption

This is where the semantic layer extends beyond traditional BI. AI/ML models require their own "semantic understanding" of data — feature stores, training datasets, inference pipelines — and the integration between SAP's data platforms and Databricks creates a bridge between descriptive/diagnostic analytics (traditional BI) and predictive/prescriptive analytics (AI/ML).

Business Data Cloud — Business Products & Intelligent Applications

The latest evolution — SAP Business Data Cloud — represents the culmination of SAP's semantic layer journey. Business Data Cloud is the integrated data foundation that brings together:

  • SAP Datasphere for data integration, modeling, and semantic governance
  • SAP Databricks for advanced analytics, AI/ML, and data engineering
  • Business Content and Business Products — prebuilt, SAP-curated data models and semantic definitions aligned to SAP business processes (procurement, finance, supply chain, HR)

Intelligent Applications built on Business Data Cloud leverage the semantic layer not just for human consumption (dashboards, reports) but for AI agent consumption — AI systems that can understand business context, query data semantically, and take automated actions.

This is the semantic layer's ultimate evolution: from human abstraction layer to AI abstraction layer. The same semantic definitions that help a business user understand revenue by region now help an AI agent understand what "revenue by region" means in context, how to query it, and what actions to recommend based on the results.

SAP's Strategic Direction: Business Suite in the Cloud & AI First

Across all these technologies, SAP's strategic direction is clear and focused:

Priority remains Business Suite in the Cloud and AI First.

This means:

  1. Cloud-native is the primary investment vector. Datasphere, SAC, HANA Cloud, Business Data Cloud, and Databricks integration represent the future of SAP analytics and data management.
  2. AI is embedded, not bolted on. From SAP Joule (the AI copilot) to predictive planning in SAC to custom ML in Databricks, AI is becoming a first-class citizen across the analytics stack.
  3. Hybrid scenarios are supported and respected. SAP recognizes that essential on-premise data management layers — BW/4HANA, S/4HANA, even legacy BW — will continue to operate for years to come. The cloud-native applications are designed to coexist with and enhance on-premise systems, not immediately replace them. Live connections, data replication, federation, and integration services ensure that on-premise data is accessible and valuable within the cloud analytics ecosystem.

Our Commitment to Education and Enablement

As someone who has spent 26 years in this space — living through every platform shift, every tool migration, every strategic pivot — I understand deeply that technology transitions are also people transitions. New platforms are only as powerful as the people who can use them effectively.

Our education priority will align with SAP's BI strategy of Business Suite in the Cloud with an AI focus:

  • For cloud and AI: We are investing heavily in training content for Datasphere, SAC, HANA Cloud, Business Data Cloud, Databricks integration, and AI/ML capabilities. This is where the future is, and this is where our education roadmap is concentrated.
  • For customers who still need to support on-premise solutions like BusinessObjects 2025, we remain committed to your success. We will continue to offer free e-learning learning journeys through learning.sap.com, covering Universe design (UNX), Web Intelligence, Crystal Reports, and other on-premise BI tools. Free training will continue to be offered via learning.sap.com with the support of learning groups — communities of practice where learners can collaborate, ask questions, and share knowledge.
  • For deeper enablement, our paid subscription to the SAP Learning Hub provides access to expert-led live sessions and practice systems — invaluable resources for maintaining mission-critical BI on-premise solutions while gradually building competency in cloud and AI technologies. These resources are designed to help existing customers bridge the gap between their current on-premise reality and their cloud and AI future.

Looking Ahead: 2026 and Beyond

The future is strong and focused on SAP's Cloud and AI strategy. I look forward to our team's collaboration as we navigate this next chapter together.

In 2025 and 2026, we are embracing AI not only as a subject we teach but as a tool that transforms how we teach:

  • AI-Assisted Certification — the Get Certified program is being rolled out in 2025, leveraging AI to create more adaptive, personalized, and rigorous certification experiences.
  • AI-Enhanced Content Development — we are exploring and implementing newer ways of developing educational content using AI, from intelligent content generation to adaptive learning paths to AI-powered practice environments.

2026 is the new beginning — a new era of continuous development and delivery of educational solutions to help customers, partners, and practitioners succeed with SAP's Cloud and AI strategy.

Together, through collaboration and a relentless commitment to seeking new ways to offer better training using AI, we will ensure that the next generation of SAP BI professionals is equipped for a world where the semantic layer is intelligent, the data is in the cloud, and AI is woven into every decision.

The semantic layer has come a long way from a .UNV file connecting to an Oracle database in 1998. But its core purpose hasn't changed: make data understandable, accessible, and actionable for the people — and now the AI systems — that need it. I'm grateful to have been part of this journey, and I'm energized by what's ahead.
